1: Ki et-kol-zeh natati el-libbi u-levarer et-kol-zeh — 'I laid all this to heart and considered it carefully.' The verb berar (H1305, to sift/test/purify/clarify) — the same word as 3:18's 'God is testing them'; here the Teacher applies the testing/clarifying process to his own observations. Asher ha-tzadiqim ve-ha-chakhamim u-ma'aseihem be-yad ha'Elohim — 'that the righteous and the wise and all they do are in God's hands.' Despite all the observed inversions and futilities, this is the Teacher's theological anchor: all humans and their works are in God's hands (yad ha'Elohim). Gam-ahavah gam-sin'ah ein yode'a ha'adam — 'yet no one knows whether love or hatred awaits them.' Even within God's hands, the specific destiny — divine love or divine anger — is unknown to the human. The epistemological limit applies even to the most basic question of one's standing before God.
2: Hakol ka'asher le-khol miqreh echad la-tzadiq vela-rasha — 'The same fate comes to everyone: to the righteous and to the wicked.' Miqreh echad (one fate/event) — the key phrase from 2:14 and 3:19 returns: the common death that equalizes all distinctions. The Teacher now catalogs the pairs that are equalized: righteous/wicked, good/clean/unclean, sacrificer/non-sacrificer, those who swear oaths/those who avoid them. The catalog is comprehensive — every social and religious distinction collapses at death. Ha-tov ka-chote — 'the good person and the sinner' — the broadest moral categories, both meeting the same miqreh.
3: Zeh ra be-kol asher-na'asah tahat hashemesh ki-miqreh echad le-khol — 'This is the evil in everything done under the sun: one fate comes to all.' The ra (evil) of the one fate is the Teacher's moral assessment of death's indiscriminate character. Ve-gam lev benei ha-adam male ra — 'Moreover, people's hearts are full of evil.' This is not a new observation but its intensification: the ra (evil) of the one fate corresponds to the ra (evil) in human hearts. Ve-holeylot be-lehavhem be-chayyeihem — 'and madness fills them throughout their lives.' Holalot (madness/raving) from halal (to be mad/praise wildly) — the human condition under the sun is madness. Ve-acharav el-ha-metim — 'and after that, they go to the dead.' The simple temporal statement: after life, death. No elaboration, no comfort.
4: Ki mi asher yevuchar el kol-ha-chayyim yesh bitachon — 'Anyone still among the living has hope.' The word bitachon (H986, hope/trust/confidence) — the same root as batach (to trust/rely on), frequently a trust-in-God word (Ps 22:4-5; Prov 3:5). Here it describes the hope that belongs to the living simply by virtue of being alive. Ki-kelev chai hu tov min-ha-ari ha-met — 'for a living dog is better off than a dead lion.' The proverb inverts status: the dog was the most despised animal in the ANE; the lion the most noble. But alive/dead reverses the hierarchy completely. Life — any life — has the advantage of bitachon; death has none.
5: Ki ha-chayyim yod'im she-yamutu — 'The living know that they will die.' This knowledge of one's own mortality is the human's unique self-awareness — the animal equivalent of the olam planted in the human heart (3:11). Ve-ha-metim einam yod'im me'umah — 'but the dead know nothing.' The Teacher's statement about the dead's knowledge is within his 'under the sun' epistemological frame — he does not claim to know what lies beyond death (cf. 3:21: 'who knows?'). From the perspective of earthly existence, the dead are absent from everything that happens under the sun. Ve-ein od lahem sakar — 'they have no further reward' — sakar (H7939, wage/reward) — the dead receive no more compensation for their former work.
6: Gam ahavah gam sin'ah gam qin'atam kebar avadah — 'Their love, their hatred, and their envy have all vanished.' The three driving forces of human social life — love, hatred, envy (qin'ah, 4:4) — have perished (avad, to be lost/destroyed) with the dead. Ve-chelek ein-lahem od le-olam — 'they have no share any longer in anything that happens under the sun.' Chelek (H2506, portion/share/lot) — the same word as helqi (my portion, 2:10): the dead have no portion in the ongoing life of the world. This is not a statement about the afterlife but about the dead's absence from earthly participation.
7: Lekh ekhol be-simhah lachmekha — 'Go, eat your food with gladness.' The imperatives of the carpe diem counsel are now at their most direct: lekh (go), ekhol (eat), shtei (drink — from the next line). Ki-kebar ratzah ha'Elohim et-ma'asekha — 'for God has already approved what you do.' Ratzah (H7521, to be pleased with/accept/approve) — the theological grounding of the carpe diem: God's prior approval of the person's enjoyment is already secured. The 'already' (kebar) is significant: the divine approval is not conditional on finding the right moments; it is established in advance. The person who eats gladly is not choosing against God's will — they are acting within it.
8: Be-khol-et yih'yu begadekha levanim — 'Let your clothing always be fresh and white.' Levanim (white) — the garments of celebration and purity in the ANE (also connected to priestly garments and the eschatological white garments of Rev 3:5; 7:9). Ve-shemen al-roshekha al-yechsar — 'and let your head not lack for oil.' Anointing oil on the head was a sign of festivity and honor (Ps 23:5; 45:7; Amos 6:6). The counsel: present yourself as a celebrant at the feast of life, not as a mourner withholding joy. The carpe diem posture is a bodily as well as internal reality.
9: Re'eh chayyim im-ha-ishah asher-ahavta — 'Enjoy life with the wife you love.' Re'eh (see/enjoy/find good in) — the Teacher's characteristic verb now applied to the marital relationship: enjoy the life you share with the wife you love. Kol-yemei chayyei havlekha — 'throughout all your fleeting days under the sun — all the days of your vapor.' The phrase chayyei havlekha (the life of your vapor) — used in 6:12 for the duration of the vapor-life. Ki hu helqekha ba-chayyim — 'for that is your portion in this life.' Helqekha — the positive counsel's key word: the marital joy is the helq that actually belongs to the person, as real a portion as any material acquisition.
10: Kol asher timtza yadkha la'asot be-kochakha aseh — 'Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with all your strength.' The intensifying phrase be-kochakha (with your strength/power) — the same radical intensity with which the Teacher tested wisdom and pleasure is now applied to the carpe diem counsel: do fully what is before you. Ki ein ma'aseh ve-cheshbon ve-da'at ve-chakhmah bi-she'ol asher atah holekh sham — 'there is no work, no planning, no knowledge, no wisdom in the grave — where you are going.' The fourfold negation in Sheol (ma'aseh=work, cheshbon=planning/reckoning, da'at=knowledge, chakhmah=wisdom) is the counterpart to the Teacher's fourfold investigative verbs in 8:16. Everything that constitutes the active life under the sun is absent in Sheol: urgency is therefore appropriate now.
11: Shavti ve-ra'oh tahat hashemesh — 'Again I observed that under the sun.' The empirical formula introduces the famous observation about time and chance. Lo la-qallim ha-merutz ve-lo la-giborim ha-milchamah ve-gam lo la-chakhamim lechem — 'the race is not won by the swift, nor the battle by the strong, nor bread by the wise.' Three pairs of expectation reversed: the swift runner, the mighty warrior, the wise person — none of these attributes guarantees the corresponding outcome. Ki et ve-fega yiqreh et-kulam — 'rather, time and chance happen to them all.' Et (time/moment) + fega (H6294, occurrence/chance/accident) — the two-word phrase for the element of contingency that disrupts predictable outcomes. Fega is a rare word (used only here in the OT); its rarity emphasizes the singularity of the observation.
12: Ki gam lo-yeda ha'adam et-ito — 'No one knows when their hour will come.' Ito (his time/hour) — the appointed moment that et ve-fega (v.11) unpredictably delivers. Ke-dagim she-ne'achazim be-metzodah ra'ah — 'as fish are caught in a cruel net.' Metzodah (H4686, net/hunting device) — the same word used for the wicked woman's heart in 7:26 (a trap). The net/trap metaphor: the fish does not know when the net will close; the bird does not know when the snare will spring. Ke-hem yukashim livnei ha-adam le-et ra'ah — 'so people are trapped at some evil moment when it suddenly falls upon them.' Et ra'ah (evil time/hour) — the moment of catastrophe that arrives unexpectedly, as death arrives unexpectedly in the human condition.
13: Gam-zot ra'iti chakhmah tahat hashemesh ve-gedolah hi elai — 'I also observed this example of wisdom under the sun, and it struck me as remarkable.' The Teacher introduces a narrative illustration. Ve-gedolah hi elai — 'it struck me as remarkable' — literally 'and it was great to me.' The word gedolah (great/remarkable) signals that what follows is a significant case study, despite its brevity. The narrative of the poor wise man who saved a city and was forgotten is one of Ecclesiastes' most penetrating parables of wisdom's limits.
14: Ir qetanah u-va'anashim bah me'at — 'There was a small city with few people in it.' The smallness of the city and the fewness of its people establish the vulnerability of what is defended. U-va-eleyha melek gadol — 'and a powerful king came against it.' The asymmetry: a great king against a small city. Ve-savav otah u-vanah aleha metzodim gedolim — 'besieged it, and built great siege works around it.' Metzodim gedolim (great siege works) — the full military investment of a major power against a small city. The situation appears hopeless.
15: U-matza vah ish misken chakham — 'But a poor wise man was found in the city.' Misken (H4542, poor/destitute) — a word unique to Ecclesiastes in the OT; it designates poverty as a social status. U-milat-hu et-ha'ir be-chakhmato — 'and by his wisdom he saved it.' The wisdom of the poor man accomplishes what military strength could not. Ve-adam lo zakar et-ha'ish ha-misken ha-hu — 'yet no one remembered that poor man afterward.' The memorial failure is the point: the poor wise man who saved the city receives no zikaron (remembrance). The absence of memorial repeats the pattern of 1:11 (no remembrance of former people) but now it is unjust forgetting, not merely the structural limit of memory.
16: Va-omar ani tovah chakhmah mi-gevurah — 'Then I concluded: wisdom is better than strength.' The tov min comparison is the Teacher's verdict: the poor wise man's salvation of the city is conclusive evidence of wisdom's superiority over force. Ve-chokhemat ha-misken bezuyah u-devarav einam nishma'im — 'But the poor man's wisdom is despised and his words go unheard.' The tension is not resolved: wisdom is objectively better, yet practically despised when it resides in poverty. The bezuyah (despised) echoes the royal treatment of David's wisdom before he was king (1 Sam 17:33). Poverty is the social condition that makes wisdom invisible.
17: Divrei chakhamim be-nachat nishma'im — 'Words spoken quietly by the wise carry more weight.' Be-nachat (H5183, in quietness/calmly) — nachat appeared at 4:6 and 6:5 for the rest that is better than restless striving. Now it describes the delivery mode of wisdom: quietly, without shouting. Mi-ze'aqat moshe ba-kesilim — 'than the shouting of a ruler among fools.' The ruler (moshel, H4910) whose audience is fools produces shouting, not wisdom. The contrast: quiet speech among attentive hearers outweighs loud speech among fools who do not hear.
18: Tovah chakhmah mi-klei milchamah — 'Wisdom is better than weapons of war.' The final tov min comparison of this section extends the poor wise man's lesson to a principle. Ve-choteh echad ye'abed tovah harbeh — 'but one fool can undo a great deal of good.' Ye'abed (H6) from abad (to destroy/cause to perish) — the single sinner's destructive capacity. The asymmetry is deliberately stark: wisdom of many is better than weapons of many, but one fool can destroy what wisdom builds. This is the Teacher's unsentimental conclusion about the fragility of wisdom's achievements within the 'under the sun' frame — a single point of failure can unravel everything.
